transcript.text: Hello, hello, video request, video request, whoa.
So this one's from Gypsy Hustle.
It came a few days ago, but I didn't get around to actually, like, watching the videos about it.
So it took me a couple days to do it, but it goes,
Request, do you think America would be much different today had 9-11 not occurred?
What do you think has changed between life pre- and post-9-11?
And also, what do you think about the argument of the NSA spying on Americans as unconstitutional?
Isn't there a good point to be made that at the time of the drafting of the Constitution,
There didn't pose the threat of nuclear weapons in the hands of religious extremists?
Comparing the threats of today versus those of 1776, we see obvious differences.
If the U.S. can stop attacks, then do you believe it's true?
We can sacrifice privacy for security, thanks.
Wow, Gypsy Hustle gives lots of good requests.
Yeah, keep bringing them, Gypsy Hustle.
You bring the good ones.
Okay, so, do you think America would be much different today had 9-11 not occurred?
Oh yeah, oh yeah, the world's big time different.
9-11 big time changed the world, man.
It kind of sped stuff up.
It brought us closer to World War III.
Well, it caused the war in Iraq.
Back when we went into, I know the majority of us wanted to do Afghanistan,
And I think even when we first went into Iraq, it was 69% I saw in a Time magazine poll,
You know, like a month after it happened.
It could have been Afghanistan, but, you know, people are down with that.
And even as far as the Edward Snowden thing, it's about split down the middle.
I just saw the Pierce Morgan thing.
Yeah, he says split right down the middle.
Well, half the people think what Edward Snowden did was wrong, and half the people think what Edward Snowden did was right.
And this is even after, you know, we found out, you know, that the government was doing it against people's knowledge, which is not good.
So, I mean, if they had done it above board since the beginning, it would be higher than 50%, and we'd be doing it anyway.
You know what I mean?
So you don't have to do it secretively.
People would allow themselves to get to be spied on like those men, if you convince them that, you know, they could deter nuclear annihilation.
I mean, it's true.
If you can have all of these, if you can get your hands on, like, nuclear weapons, I think, like, hundreds of nuclear, dirty nuclear weapons have been, like, missing from Russia, you know?
The Soviet Union collapsed in, like, what was it, 1991 or something like that?
And ever since then, they've kind of, they didn't need all those nuclear weapons.
They had thousands and thousands and thousands of them, and Russia's a poor country.
There's lots of poor scientists, poor people who have access to that kind of stuff who would sell it.
And, you know, I mean, North Korea has a nuclear weapon.
They did their nuclear test.
They shot a satellite up into space, you know?
And everybody knows how crazy they are, but they got a scientist guy in there to build him a nuclear weapon.
And so it's totally believable that you could, that terrorists could get together and communicate and organize and get themselves one of these dirty nukes, you know, that could take out a whole city.

And, you know, they're only, like, as big as a suitcase, somehow smuggle them into America, you know, under a car through Mexico or Canada or something, and kaboom, kaplowey, DC, or even do it something even more coordinated, you know?
Maybe they could even have a hundred of them, and they could kaplowey all the big cities in America and take America out, take America back to the Stone Age.
And so it's definitely, you know, there's something to be said for the government being able to just spy on anybody and everybody.
And, you know, having all that access to people's emails and stuff so that they could, you know, know exactly what the terrorists are doing and thwart the attack.
The government said that they had stopped a lot of attacks like that.
But, you know, that's not really what we're talking, that's not the whole story, though.
That's not really what the whole Edward Snowden thing is about.
So the whole picture, ooh.
Ooh, but, so 9-11 caused all of that, caused people, and especially the government, to kind of be more, take this whole anti-terrorism stuff more seriously.
And so that's how 9-11 changed the world, man, you know, big time.
And it also showed us the attitudes of everybody.
It kind of brought, it brought the subconscious of everybody up to the surface.
You know, I mean, right after 9-11, we found out, oh, everybody loves America.
Everybody's like, oh, sorry about 9-11.
And then we attack a couple of countries, and then everybody's like, no, no, that's not what the idea is, you know.
And a lot of Americans are against that, too.
So we kind of find out that, oh, we kind of overreacted.

And, oh, personally, what I think what happened with 9-11 is the government has been spying on, has been using high technology and spying on terrorists and all this kind of stuff before 9-11, you know.
They've been doing it for a while, and I think they knew that the terrorist attack was coming.
And there's even evidence of that.
I don't know the details of it, but, yeah, there's actual proof that they knew that there was an attack.
Everybody expected attack on the, I mean, not just the government, dude.
Everybody who knew what was going on is expected attack on the World Trade Center.
So I remember when I was in 97, I was in a bar in Budapest hanging out with these Canadian soldiers who were on leave from the war in Bosnia.
There was a war going on in, like, Bosnia and Serbia at the time.
And he was like, dude, you don't understand.
Everybody hates you because they were fighting Muslims.
Everybody hates you.
And I'm telling you, they will take down the World Trade Center.
Those World Trade Centers are going to go down.
And he reminded me of, you know, the bomb that went off in 93 in the World Trade Center that was almost successful and almost brought it down.
So what I think would happen is the government just was spying on these terrorists, and they knew that they were going to do that attack,
And they knew what day it was, and they knew all about it.
And so they just went in ahead of time, and they set up those explosives on those buildings to make it so that the buildings would actually collapse at the rate of actual free fall,
Which never happens when you see buildings collapse, you know, like when you see buildings collapse, like when they collapse big hotels in Las Vegas and stuff,
Those buildings collapse was, like, not even as well done as how well the World Trade Centers collapsed.
And those things have explosives all over their, like, you know, vital areas.
So a plane flying into a World Trade Center and, like, exploding isn't going to collapse one of those buildings.
It's just going to cause a fire.
Those buildings, and people film those things, film explosions happening right before it was coming down.
It was all timed.
And you could see the windows getting blown out before it came down, and then there's, you know, and then the other building next door fell down.
So maybe they expected another plane to hit the other building, Building 7, and then they accidentally detonated or something, but it collapsed.
Like, how could a building next to another building collapse and make the other building next to it collapse?
It doesn't make any sense.
So I think, yeah, 9-11 was an inside job in that regard, you know.
And when Ahmadinejad says that the real bin Laden is in America, you know, that's a valid point, you know.
I mean, you're saying bin Laden, the dude who did 9-11, well, it was kind of a, you know, it wasn't the terrorists who placed all those bombs on the building itself.
So, so I think they wanted, you know, they wanted people to go, they wanted us to attack Iraq in order to get, in order to, you know, to try to get their hands on the oil, get control of the oil.
So, somehow, they thought that would work, you know.
Now they're contracting out to, like, other countries, France and China and stuff, instead of America.
They're not thankful and they're pissed off.
But, and also they wanted to keep that, you know, military-industrial complex going, keep those guys making money.
Halliburton making money.
Who is that guy?
I know I'm taking the 9-11 thing a little bit, like, I'm getting really detailed into it.
But I'll move on to the next one.
But who is it?
The vice president, Cheney, owned 4 million shares in Halliburton, which was the big subcontractor that went in there and made a ton of money.
Off of the war in Iraq where they would charge, like, 150 bucks for a load of laundry and they wouldn't even clean, they'd destroy your clothes instead of clean them.
And then they made it so that the soldiers had to, were mandatory, had to clean their clothes there when they just wanted to do it with their hands.
Because, you know, it made the Halliburton money and all this kind of crazy stuff.
And he used to be, like, the president of Halliburton, too.
But anyways, it's called Academy now.
But, so, yeah, dude, that's what I think 9-11 was all about.
And so that they could also, you know, have more of that military presence around the world to try to go after the Middle Easterners.

I think there's this big, like, fundamentalist Christian, like Armageddon, you know, the devil's in the desert, defeating them, you know, type of a, carrying out the prophecy in the Bible type of a scenario, you know.
Where they just, yeah, they were like, yeah, let's just kill all those Muslims and all the terrorists and show everybody how crazy they are.
But it's kind of backfiring.
Now people don't like America because of that.
But anyways, that's what I think 9-11.
And so that kind of brought all that forward.
It gave, you know, the U.S. government more power to do this whole wiretapping stuff.
Okay, so what do you think has changed between life pre- and post-9-11?
Life, well, yeah, it caused us to get more, well, I mean, it killed.
It caused a whole lot of people to have, like, friends and family killed by those wars.
Was it like 5,000 Americans killed in Iraq, a whole bunch killed in Afghanistan, hundreds of thousands killed in Iraq?
It's changed American life in that regard.
It cost a whole lot of money, so it screwed up our economy big time.
And how did it change life in Iraq?
I think it did bring Iraq closer to democracy.
I think overall it had, I mean, I'm not going to say a positive effect on Iraq because Iraq could have changed anyways.
And they could have changed more peacefully, and I think peaceful changes and revolutions is better than ones where all those people die.
But, I mean, the Iraq today is a lot freer than it was under Saddam.
You know, you get to have people get away with a lot more than they did then.
You know, you get to have the motorbike gangs riding around, and, you know, there's more westernization.
There's a lot of people there who are thankful for America still, and a lot of people aren't.
So it brought stuff forward.
I think, you know, and Iraq has still got problems and stuff, but I think it brought them up.
You know, overall I think Iraq is better off than they were then.
But it wasn't worth it.
It definitely wasn't worth all that money and all that death.
Okay, so, also, and then somebody might be like, what?
What do you mean Iraq is better off now?
All those people died.
Well, you know, people die, you know.
I mean, people, that's kind of how life is over there, you know.
Change the world through blood.
I mean, if they were going to have a revolution, people would have died anyways.
There's that many people, so.
And sure, they'd get another Saddam Hussein in there, but it's not as bad as before.
I think it's a little bit better.
Okay, so anyways.
Also, what do you think about the argument that the NSA spying on Americans is unconstitutional?
Well, let's read the U.S. Constitution, the Fourth Constitution.
This is the one on my website.
It says,
The right of the people to be secure in their persons, houses, papers, and effects against unreasonable searches and seizures
Shall not be violated and no warrants shall issue, but upon probable cause, supported by oath or affirmation,
And particularly describing the place to be searched and the persons or things to be seized.
So, the government says that it doesn't violate the Constitution.
And, you know, and then a lot of other people in America think that it does violate the Constitution.
So, I think the argument that it does not violate the Constitution is that they are doing – they're not actually going in to –
They're not actually going in and, like, a person is not – an actual human being is not listening to your phone calls or reading your emails.
And obviously, obviously, there's 300 million Americans.
How could anybody – how could he – it's impossible.
It couldn't happen.
But they're still gathering it, okay?
So, that's the argument.
So, I guess the argument that it's not – doesn't violate the Fourth Amendment is that nobody's reading it, you know?
Like, they're like, what?
No, in 1776, they were, like, actually reading it, you know?
And that was a violation.
But, like, nobody's reading it.
Only our computers are.
And then the people who think that it is – does violate the Constitution are like, well, you know, they have it now, you know?
And so my opinion is that it does violate the Fourth Amendment because they're collecting it, you know?
Whether it's a computer collecting your emails and your phone calls, you know, or a human being, like, walking in there and grabbing it with their hands, you know?
And they still have it, you know?
I mean, it's like in 1776, they could go into your – somebody would go into your house and take all your crap just because you're an American.
And that's what they're reading it.
Obviously, it's unconstitutional.
That's the point.
They took it.
It doesn't matter if they actually read it or not, you know?
They have it now and they can read it if they want.
And that's how the government – that's what the government's doing.
They have it and they can read it.
And what Edward Snowden was saying is that they are – they're collecting it on everybody, okay, every single person.
And they put it in this big database that they – of every single person that they can then go back in time and, you know, cherry pick all the stuff that you did and use that against you.
And it's called a dragnet where they just go through everybody and he says that – and obviously that's a clear violation of the Fourth Constitution, like I said before.
But he says that, you know, if you do that, it could even get worse because then you could – it kind of sets this precedent.

It sets this – Edward Snowden called it a policy, you know, that they have where – and then if they – if there's like another terrorist attack, you know, where they do another Line 11 where they allow another terrorist attack to go because – you know, and this has happened a lot of times before when the government allows this kind of stuff to happen.
You know, they blew up the main to start this U.S.-Spanish War.
The Gulf of Tonkin incident was all prearranged so that they could go into Vietnam.
I mean, we are – and we've – and it's – that's common knowledge.
They've admitted it to it.
So, you know, I bet you in 50 years they'll admit to the 9-11 thing too.
It's just part of the policy.
And so – so, yeah.
So what if there's like another 9-11 type of a thing and then they go – and then they're like, oh, no, now it's even more serious.
And now you've got to – now we've got to actually, you know, go after all of the Muslims and now they're all bad, you know, and then what, arrest all of them?
I mean – and we have a history of doing that too.
When World War II happened, they went and – they started all these concentration camps for Japanese-Americans, you know.
It was ridiculous just because they're – they happen to be – we have – that's what America is.
It's a big melting pot of America, of the world, you know.
That was a big embarrassment for America.
And then, of course, the whole hippie thing.
You've got all 500,000 hippies in jail for like – in prison, you know, for like – you know.
The prison sentence for drugs, for like magic mushrooms, you know, which grows out of the ground is like worse than rape, you know.
You're in jail.
You're fucked.
You're in jail for life.
And we've got half a million people in there.
It costs – we spend $40 billion a year on the war on drugs.
It costs just an ungodly amount of money.
And so, yeah, so like, yeah, could it happen again, you know, like if she gets – like, say an American city actually does get nuked.
You know how, like, out of control it could get?
Like, then you could start doing dragnets and, like, you know, locking up all the Mexicans, locking up all the Muslims and, oh, my God, you know, concentration camps all over the place.
They'll put those FEMA camps to use that they've built.
You can see them.
They filmed them.
You go to the FEMA camp and I saw that it was Alex Jones and Jesse Ventura went there and they were filming them.

They were actually filmed the coffins stacked up, like, 12 high, 12 feet high, you know, 20 coffins high and plastic ones and rows and rows and rows and rows of them and these giant barbed wire fences and stuff, you know.
And they're all over the place.
There's, like, hundreds of them.
So, like, it's like, you know, it's like they're already planning on it because they already built up the freaking infrastructure.
So, anyways, but that's the real issue here.
Well, let's actually pass through all the questions and then I'll talk about that.
Isn't there a good point to be made that at the time of the drafting of the Constitution there didn't pose a threat of nuclear weapons in the hands of religious extremists?
Yeah.
Comparing the threats of today versus those of 76, we see obvious differences.
Yeah.
And that's what people, that's what the government should have tried to explain to the populace.
The problem is that they didn't.
They were doing all that kind of stuff against the knowledge of the U.S. population and the sad thing is that they didn't have to.
Like, why were they doing that?

Because now that this whole Snowden thing comes out, half of America and actually most of the people, I guess a little bit over half of the people in Pierce Morgan's audience there raising their hands, said that Snowden was a bad guy.
You know, he shouldn't have leaked that information and so that the government does have the right to spy on us without us even knowing.

So if they think that, I mean, imagine how many of Americans would be supporting that the government has a right to spy on us like that, like the way that they say they're with our knowledge, you know?
Like, it's like, why do they have to be secretive?
And that's where the problem came in, comes in, because that shows that they'll, you know, then, like, that they'll do other stuff that they don't care what America thinks.
It's not a democratic process, and that's what this country is supposed to be, a democratic country.
So let's just finish really quick.
Yeah, obviously there was, okay, if the NASA can, if the NSA can stop attacks, then do you believe it's true we can sacrifice privacy for security?
Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah, I think it's great to sacrifice privacy for security.
The problem is that they should, and you know what, the thing is, you're not sacrificing your privacy for security.
Nobody's going to read your emails.
Nobody's going to, even if the government has it, like, locked up in a computer, nobody's going to read it, and nobody's going to see it.
It doesn't matter.
So your privacy is still, you know, in a real way secure, but not, you know, you, but, you know, not, not, it's that people have a lot more access to it.

Um, see, I am a lot different from your average person in this particular subject because, you know, I'm the guy who, like, I upload videos and I talk about, like, daily vlogs, talk about what I'm doing.

I'm really open about my life, and, you know, I'm not totally open because I have family members who tell me I can't say certain things, you know, like, you know, like, money type of stuff and, like, you know, where my apartment is and that kind of stuff.
But, um, and, you know, and, like, film, I can't film them or talk to them or talk about them or anything like that.
But as far as I'm concerned, I'm open.
I'm an open book because I think that the more open we all are, the more, like, anonymous we can all be, you know.
I mean, when, when all those college streakers, you know, run naked through college, you know, they're basically have their clothes on because they're all, they're all naked, you know what I mean?

Like, you're all, like, like, when you have mob, mob rule and the mob attacks the Versailles palace, like, everybody's, they can all run in and desecrate the Versailles because they're all together, you know, they're all anonymous that way, you know.
And so, um, if we were all open, then it wouldn't, you know, it wouldn't, they, the government could just go in and go after the terrorists, you know.
I don't think people in the government are, uh, you know, malicious.
I think they just, um, I just think, um, generally, but the problem is, see, this is the problem.
Um, Edward Snowden, what Edward Snowden did was good.
Edward Snowden is not, is a, is a, is a good guy because we were doing that against, against, uh, against, um, Americans' knowledge.
And they even denied it.
Before Edward Snowden came out, this is a ridiculous thing about the whole situation.
Before, before Edward Snowden, uh, became a whistleblower, the government was actually denying doing it.
They have, they have, um, you know, I, cause I watch the Young Turks and the Young Turks are really good about actually, um, collecting clips.
If I had the time, I'd love to do that.
Start a YouTube channel and just show all the clips of the, but there's all, all other YouTube channels.
If I had the time, what I should do is just collect, like, playlists and stuff and label it and stuff.
But, but, but yeah, they, they showed clips of, uh, you know, uh, congressmen asking the NSA guy if they do that, you know, cause they were suspecting.
And then straight out denying it, you know, and suddenly you can't have a conversation about it.
Now Edward Snowden, um, has, was, had all of these really high level, um, really high level, um, excess.
And he saw that, um, he saw that, um, you know, what they could do, you know, like, and, and what he could do and what people, and, and he just, he saw, and he saw the trend.
He's like, from his point of view, he saw the trend changing.
And he saw, um, the, the, the intelligence gathering, you know, getting more and more sophisticated and more and more, um, more and more, um, uh, you know, in depth.

And, and, uh, and, and, and he, and he says, what Edward Snowden says is that you could, um, the government can, you know, like, see, look, if they, if they do all this kind of stuff without anybody knowing about it, and, and then you have, you have, and all these people are appointed and they'll make the decisions for you.
Like they're doing it secretively.
And of course they, they say it's unconstitutional because their courts say it's unconstitutional and they don't even tell you why they just say, oh, what, don't worry about it.
We, our courts say it's, it's, it's constitutional, um, you know, and then there's like a really, like, so it becomes really secretive.
And so there's a small number of people who have access to this kind of stuff, then they can have the power.
And so if they decide that they don't like somebody, you know, like say, like say in the future, like five or 10 years or whatever, uh, regular people can run for president.
Okay.
Um, and, uh, you know, so that, of course that's going to scare the shit out of all of these.
Those, uh, establishment people.
Um, and, and so they can't fight back, you know, with money, you know, with all their super PAC money and all that kind of stuff.
So now what can they do?
Well, they can, if, if some, if some like really good, like me, you know, or some, or some like really, really good, you know, regular person does their homework and runs for president.
Cause I really want to make a difference.
Well, then they can go in on that information with all the information that they have gathered on them and cherry pick.
And then they can, they can say, Oh, we have this information about them.
We have that information about them.
And they can be really murky about where they got it because they can, because they're really secretive anyway.
And then they can, they can paint a picture, um, however they want about anybody.
And they can smear whoever they want.
And they can like, like a sniper that can just say whatever they want about anybody.

I mean, if you're going to, like, if you, if you even went through my YouTube videos, um, and you had like a workforce of a hundred thousand people or whatever, you know, to go through my, or, or even if he was one guy and he spent a lot of time going through all of my videos, you could make me out to be a psycho.
You know, you could, you could clip this little bit of thing I say, cause I make blanket statements a lot, you know, but they're in a context, this, this, this.
And then you can make a video of Kyle pounds is crazy.
You watch and then people would be like, Oh my God, crazy, crazy.
Oh my God.
You know what I mean?
So they can do that.
And that's, and so that's, and so when it's all secret and it's not above board and, and explain to people, um, um, you can, uh, it's not fair.
You know, you're, you're, you're back to not having a level playing field and see the, the, the whole fact that they, that they were denying it and see her.
Oh, here's the ridiculous part though.
This is the totally ridiculous part is that they were, they were denying that they were ever doing it.
Right.
And now Edward Snowden comes out and they have to admit it.
And so now they're like, well, we welcome to the debate.
Let's debate about it.
Well, why didn't you welcome to the debate before?
And, oh, and at the same time, they're like, yeah, we welcome to the debate.
Uh, but that guy's a traitor.
We need to throw him in jail forever.
It's like, well, if you welcome to the debate, like, why didn't you make, why didn't you let everybody know that you were doing it in the first place?
Why, why can't, um, you don't welcome the debate, you know?
And if you do welcome the debate, then, then pardon Snowden and say, okay, thanks.
Thanks Snowden for allowing us to begin the debate.
Because without you, we couldn't have the debate in the first place.
And so, um, it's ridiculous, you know, you don't welcome a debate.
And then they go in and they, and they just, um, yeah.
So the saying that he's a, that he's like a traitor, or I guess the other guy on the video that I saw, he said he was a, a defector.
Defector.
Well, I mean, what do you mean?
He's a defector.
He can't move back to America.
You told him if you, if you get him.
And then the same, the next sentence, he's like, yeah, we need to lock him up forever.
Well, if he comes back to America and he's locked up forever, he has to defect somewhere.
Dumbass.
And, and so I'm good.
I'm glad he didn't call him a traitor.
Because if anybody calls Edward Snowden a traitor, it's, it's not true because a traitor is somebody who, who, who goes, who turns against your country.
He's not turning against his country.
He's, he wanted to protect Americans by saying that.
He's a, you know, so he's a hero.
Um, he goes for the transparency.
I mean, and look, and obviously he didn't, he's not.
The funny thing is also is that a lot, all these like people in power, politicians and people, the people who you see like knocking Edward Snowden.
And he's like super rich people, like correspondence on Fox News and stuff.
They do everything for money.
They're all rich and they'll do anything.
They'll, they'll lie, cheat, steal, you know, bribe, all that kind of stuff.
Well, Edward Snowden gave up a really nice life.
He was making a ton of money in Hawaii and now he has no life.
And now he's probably going to get like assassinated in a couple of years or have to live in like some other country.
He fucked himself.
So he, so he obviously did it because he thinks it's the right thing to do, not because he wanted to get rich off it.
But, so, um, you know, pot calling the kettle black, is that the saying?
But, um, yeah, so, so, uh, they shouldn't, they should have been doing that above board, you know, the whole time.

And, and, you know, and if they had been letting people know that they were doing that, then they could have talked people into, and then, and then explained that, you know, the dangers of nuclear weapons and all that kind of stuff.
They could have talked people into, um, you know, having all of their stuff like collected, all their private emails and stuff collected.
Um, but I would understand, you know, but I mean, if, if, if like most of the American public, see politicians are supposed to make decisions that the American public wants.
So if most of the American public actually didn't want that, then we should stop doing it.

We should stop, um, spying on people, you know, because people, a lot of people, it makes them uncomfortable knowing that, that, you know, all of their emails and their phone calls are recorded and stored someplace.

Um, especially, you know, in a world like this, where they're doing it secretively, because now, because, and, and we have some kind of, you know, oligarchical, um, old boys club who, who, who, who, who control.
Then they can, yeah, they can go in and just read it, you know, like maybe they can have their little fun little dinner parties and get together.
And I mean, and all like Edward Snowden could have done that.
Edward Snowden had the power, even for the president to, um, go in and, um, and, um, you know, just, just for fun, just read, read your emails and listen to your phone calls and stuff.
And I know they say, oh, we're not going to do it.
We can't do it without a warrant.
Well, is that you're probably lying.
I mean, you're lying yet again, because you were lying when you said you were, you, the program existed in the first place.
And now you have to admit that the program exists.
So you admit that the program exists, but now you don't, you don't do warrants.
So, um, yeah.
I mean, that's just the argument for why you wouldn't want to, um, have this program in the first place.
Number one, it also costs a whole bunch of money.
The, um, all of the, you know, these, uh, internet service providers and, uh, telephone service providers charge an exorbitant amount of money to, uh, to share this stuff with the government.
